ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Shish Tawook; closest defensible match is chicken kebab food product (FOODON_03302051). Dish includes mushrooms and mandi rice accompaniment not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (chicken meat, grilled) used as closest defensible anchor. Full dish includes marinade components (garlic, mustard, yoghurt), mushrooms, and mandi rice which are not individually coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for Shish Tawook as a composite dish; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: Mandi rice (basmati rice, saffron, whole spices)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Shish Tawook as served in Singapore restaurants weighs approximately 550 g, comprising grilled chicken skewers with mushrooms and a portion of mandi rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, grilled (FDC ID 331960) and Rice, white, long-grain, cooked (FDC ID 169756), combined with HPB Singapore nutrient reference for composite dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact approved-source entry exists for Shish Tawook as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central grilled chicken breast (FDC ID 331960) and cooked long-grain white rice (FDC ID 169756), weighted to reflect the dish composition (approximately 55% chicken with mushrooms and marinade, 45% mandi rice by mass). The input record values (energy 141.82 kcal, protein 11.82 g, fat 6.36 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with this blended composition and are retained as the best available estimate. Sodium at 200 mg/100 g reflects marinade and seasoning contribution. All values are treated as medium-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Basmati rice, white, boiled (GI ~58); blended with grilled chicken component (GI ~0) weighted by carbohydrate contribution.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Shish Tawook as a composite dish. GI estimated at 56 using a weighted blend: mandi rice (basmati, GI ~58 per University of Sydney GI Database) contributes the majority of digestible carbohydrate; grilled chicken and mushroom components contribute negligible carbohydrate (GI effectively 0). Weighted GI = (carbohydrate from rice fraction / total carbohydrate) × 58 ≈ 56. Glycemic load per 550 g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (56 × 55.0) / 100 = 30.8 g. Carbohydrate per serving = 10.0 g/100 g × 550 g = 55.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 200 mg/100 g; 1,100 mg per 550 g serving approaches 48% of the 2,300 mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt marination and avoid adding table salt or high-sodium condiments; the potassium contribution from garlic and mushrooms provides partial offset consistent with DASH dietary principles, but total sodium per serving warrants portion awareness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI of 56 and glycemic load of 30.8 per full 550 g serving driven by mandi rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the mandi rice portion to lower the glycemic load per meal; the high protein content (11.82 g/100 g) from lean grilled chicken slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose response, making a half-plate serving with extra salad a clinically preferable option.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.45 g/100 g), low trans fat (0.04 g/100 g), and lean grilled chicken protein profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Shish Tawook is a heart-friendly choice when prepared with olive oil and grilled rather than fried; the low saturated fat and absence of significant trans fat align with AHA dietary guidelines, though sodium intake per full serving should be monitored in patients with concurrent hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile (garlic, cumin, coriander) and low saturated fat; nightshade-derived paprika is a minor caution
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The dish's anti-inflammatory spice marinade and low saturated fat content are generally favourable for rheumatoid arthritis management; patients with solanine sensitivity should confirm paprika is excluded from the marinade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shish Tawook (also spelled Shish Taouk or Shish Tawouk) is a classic Turkish and Levantine grilled chicken skewer dish widely served across the Middle East and in Singaporean Middle Eastern restaurants. The dish consists of boneless chicken breast morsels marinated in a blend of yoghurt, garlic, mustard paste, lemon juice, olive oil, and aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, and paprika, then skewered with whole mushrooms and grilled over high heat until charred and juicy. In this Singapore restaurant version, the skewers are served alongside fragrant mandi rice — a slow-cooked basmati rice preparation infused with saffron, cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves. The dish delivers a balanced macronutrient profile per 100 g: approximately 141.8 kcal, 11.8 g protein, 6.4 g fat (of which only 1.45 g is saturated), and 10.0 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 200 mg. The high protein content from lean grilled chicken supports satiety and blunts postprandial glucose response, making it a moderate-GI option (estimated GI 56) suitable for most dietary patterns when portion size is managed. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets and is free from gluten. Clinically, it is a low-cardiovascular-risk meal due to its lean protein and minimal saturated fat, though the full 550 g serving contributes approximately 1,100 mg sodium and a glycemic load of 30.8, which warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes. The mushrooms contribute beta-glucans and the garlic provides allicin, both of which have documented anti-inflammatory and immune-modulating properties.
common_user_queries: Shish tawook with mandi rice nutrition
Is shish tawook good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for Shish Tawook as a composite dish with mandi rice. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central grilled chicken breast and cooked basmati rice entries, weighted by estimated dish composition. The input record values from GrabFood (confidence 0.7) are consistent with this blended estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite nature of the dish and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Shish Tawook with mandi rice preparation are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. These values would require laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant preparation. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.04 g/100 g from the input record; this low value is consistent with trace trans fat from dairy (yoghurt) in the marinade and is retained as a supported estimate. 3. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate weighted from basmati rice GI (University of Sydney GI Database) and grilled chicken (GI effectively 0); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 4. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but does not resolve to an HPB nutrient composition page; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central as the approved primary source. 5. Multilingual names for this Turkish/Middle Eastern dish served in Singapore follow the Singaporean dish multilingual rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given its Singapore restaurant context. 6. Aliases consistency check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
